title: Digital Markets & Emerging Issues
content:
Session 9: Emerging Issues 
 
Swapan Dey v Union of India [2025 | NCLAT] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The appeal arose from an information filed alleging that Vifor International AG, the patent holder of Ferric Carboxymaltose 
(FCM) injections, had engaged in anti-competitive and abusive conduct.  
‚û¢‚Äã Due to Vifor‚Äôs licensing and commercial practices, FCM injections were neither accessible nor affordable to 
patients and consumers at large. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI, after examining the matter, found no violation of Sections 3 or 4.  
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI analysed the licensing agreements placed on record by Vifor and concluded that the terms did not appear 
to be one-sided or unreasonable when examined from the perspective of Section 3(4) read with Section 3(5) of the 
Act.  
‚û¢‚Äã There was no restriction on Vifor entering into additional licensing arrangements should it choose to do so. 
‚û¢‚Äã Also recognised the freedom of a patent holder to choose its trading partners, relying on its earlier decision in In re: 
Hiveloop and Britannia.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Held that not every entity has a right to seek access to a patented technology, unless it demonstrates that 
denial of access to an essential product or facility has resulted in adverse market effects due to a mismatch 
between supply and demand. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Aggrieved by this decision, the appellant contended that the CCI had failed to properly delineate the relevant market, assess 
dominance, and had conducted an ex ante analysis instead of a post-facto competition assessment, particularly in relation to 
alleged violations under Section 3(4). 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI has the jurisdiction to examine alleged anti-competitive conduct when the subject matter is governed by 
the Patents Act, 1970. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether licensing conditions imposed by a patentee can be examined under the Competition Act, notwithstanding the 
protection afforded by Section 3(5). 
150 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Patent Act prevails over the Competition Act in matters relating to exercise of patent rights and licensing 
conditions. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The NCLAT began by noting that the patent on FCM had expired, and therefore the drug was now in the public domain 
for manufacturing.  
‚û¢‚Äã However, the dispute related to conduct during the period when patent protection subsisted, and thus the 
interaction between the two statutes remained central. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(5) creates a statutory carve-out protecting the rights of patent holders to impose reasonable conditions necessary 
to protect their patent rights.  
‚û¢‚Äã The licensing agreements examined by the CCI did not disclose terms that could be characterised as unreasonable, 
exclusionary, or arbitrary.  
‚û¢‚Äã Crucially, there was no evidence that Vifor had imposed exclusive dealing obligations, prohibited further licensing, 
or restricted output in a manner that distorted competition. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Relying heavily on the Ericsson jurisprudence, the NCLAT reiterated that questions such as: 
‚û¢‚Äã whether licensing conditions are reasonable, 
‚û¢‚Äã whether access to a patented invention is unjustifiably denied, and 
‚û¢‚Äã whether prices are excessive in the context of patent exploitation 
‚û¢‚Äã are matters that fall squarely within the statutory scheme of the Patents Act, particularly in the context of 
compulsory licensing.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The factors that the CCI would consider under Section 19 of the Competition Act are substantially 
similar to those that the Controller of Patents is mandated to examine under Sections 84 and 89 of the 
Patents Act. 
‚óè‚Äã
This overlap is not accidental, but indicative of legislative intent: reasonable conditions imposed 
by a patentee are exempted from scrutiny under Section 3 because Parliament intended such 
issues to be resolved under the specialised patent regime.  
‚óè‚Äã
Accordingly, the Patents Act was characterised as a complete code on issues relating to: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
abuse of rights by a patentee, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
reasonableness of licensing conditions, and 
‚óÜ‚Äã
remedies such as compulsory licensing. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Applying the principle of generalia specialibus non derogant, where the subject matter concerns exercise of patent rights, the 
Patents Act prevails over the Competition Act.  
‚û¢‚Äã While both statutes may be characterised as special laws in their respective domains, the Patents Act is more specific 
with respect to patent exploitation and licensing. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Tribunal also rejected the appellant‚Äôs contention regarding failure to define the relevant market or assess dominance, 
holding that once Section 3(5) is attracted, the inquiry under Section 3 is statutorily barred insofar as reasonable 
patent-protective conduct is concerned. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(5) protects a patentee‚Äôs right to impose reasonable licensing conditions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Patent Act, 1970 prevails over the Competition Act in matters relating to the exercise of patent rights and licensing 
conditions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The CCI lacked jurisdiction to re-examine issues that fall within the exclusive domain of the Patents Act, particularly where 
no unreasonable or abusive conduct was demonstrated. 
‚ùñ‚Äã There was no violation of Sections 3 or 4 of the Competition Act. 
 
Monsanto Holdings Private Limited v  CCI  [2020 | DHC | 1J] 
Facts: 
151 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Monsanto is a global agro-biotechnology company that developed GM technology used in the manufacture of Bt cotton 
seeds.  
‚û¢‚Äã Over time, pests (bollworms) developed resistance to the existing Bt technology, necessitating the development and 
licensing of improved Bt cotton technology ‚Üí Monsanto held patents over this technology, though the technology 
in question was not an SEP. 
‚û¢‚Äã Monsanto licensed this technology through Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited (MMBL), a joint venture 
operating in the upstream market for licensing Bt cotton technology. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The downstream market consisted of seed manufacturers who sublicensed the technology from MMBL to 
produce and sell Bt cotton seeds. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The informant seed companies alleged that Monsanto Group and MMBL were dominant in the upstream market for 
licensing Bt cotton technology, enjoying nearly 99% market share, attributable to first-mover advantage and lack of viable 
substitutes at scale.  
‚û¢‚Äã Also, Monsanto Group itself had vertical presence, with approximately 14% participation in seed manufacturing, 
thereby operating both upstream and downstream. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The informants challenged certain clauses in the sub-licensing agreements entered into with MMBL. 
‚û¢‚Äã First, Clause 2.05(c) required sublicensees to notify MMBL if their affiliates entered into negotiations with any 
competitor of MMBL within 30 days of commencement.  
‚û¢‚Äã Second, Article 9.4 required sublicensees, upon termination of the agreement, to destroy all parent lines or cotton 
germplasm modified using Monsanto technology, even though such destruction could permanently eliminate 
independent R&D capabilities. 
‚ùñ‚Äã It was alleged that while Monsanto provided technology to its own group companies without imposing such onerous terms, 
independent seed manufacturers were compelled to accept these restrictive and allegedly unfair conditions.  
‚ùñ‚Äã The informants alleged violations under Sections 4(2)(a)(i) (imposition of unfair conditions), 4(2)(b) (restriction of 
technical and scientific development), 4(2)(e) (leveraging dominance), as well as Sections 3(1) and 3(4) (anti-competitive 
vertical agreements). 
‚ùñ‚Äã The CCI passed a prima facie order under Section 26(1) directing investigation.  
‚û¢‚Äã Monsanto challenged this order before the DHC, arguing lack of jurisdiction and statutory exclusion in favour of 
the Patents Act. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI has jurisdiction to examine alleged abuse of dominance and anti-competitive conduct arising from patent 
licensing arrangements governed by the Patents Act, 1970? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the decision in Bharti Airtel lays down a blanket rule requiring CCI proceedings to be deferred whenever a sectoral 
regulator exists? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the impugned licensing conditions are protected under Section 3(5) of the Competition Act as reasonable exercise 
of patent rights? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the allegations under Section 4 are merely disguised Section 3 claims, thereby excluding abuse of dominance 
scrutiny? 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Jurisdiction of CCI vis-√Ý-vis the Patents Act 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court rejected Monsanto‚Äôs argument that the Patents Act impliedly ousts CCI jurisdiction.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Relying heavily on Ericsson v. CCI, the Court reaffirmed that Section 62 of the Competition Act 
explicitly provides for concurrent applicability, making it clear that competition law supplements, rather 
than supplants, sector-specific regimes. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Patents Act governs grant, validity, and enforcement of patent rights, whereas the Competition Act scrutinises 
market conduct and competitive effects of how such rights are exercised.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
There is no irreconcilable repugnancy between the two statutes.  
152 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Abuse of patent rights, particularly when they affect market structure or competition, falls squarely 
within CCI‚Äôs mandate. 
‚û¢‚Äã Merely because conduct involves a patent does not immunise it from competition scrutiny, especially when the 
allegation is not patent infringement but market abuse through licensing practices. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Airtel is not a blanket rule: Limited domain-specific deference 
‚û¢‚Äã Monsanto‚Äôs reliance on Bharti Airtel was found to be misplaced and overbroad ‚Üí distinguished Airtel by 
examining its factual and regulatory context.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In Airtel, the dispute revolved around technical interconnection and Points of Interconnection (PoI), 
matters squarely within TRAI‚Äôs statutory expertise under Section 11 of the TRAI Act. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In contrast, the present case involved pricing, licensing terms, exclusionary conditions, and market 
foreclosure, which are core competition law questions.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Unlike TRAI, the Controller of Patents does not adjudicate on market dominance, abusive conduct, or 
competitive foreclosure. 
‚û¢‚Äã Airtel does not establish a universal rule that CCI must always defer to sectoral regulators. Deference applies only 
where: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The dispute is predominantly technical, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The regulator has exclusive statutory competence over the subject matter. 
‚û¢‚Äã Here, no such exclusivity existed ‚áí therefore, CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction was not contingent on prior findings by the 
patent authorities. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(5) Defence: Limits of IP immunity 
‚û¢‚Äã Monsanto argued that the licensing clauses were protected under Section 3(5) as reasonable conditions imposed to 
protect patent rights ‚Äì REJECTED. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Section 3(5) is not a blanket exemption, but a qualified defence limited to conditions that are necessary 
and proportionate to protect IP rights.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Clauses that: 
‚óè‚Äã
Compel disclosure of negotiations with competitors, 
‚óè‚Äã
Mandate destruction of germplasm post-termination, and 
‚óè‚Äã
Apply discriminatorily to non-group entities, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
prima facie go beyond what is reasonably necessary to prevent infringement. 
‚û¢‚Äã The right to exclude infringement cannot be stretched into a right to impose unreasonable, far-reaching, or 
market-foreclosing conditions.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Whether the conditions were reasonable is a mixed question of law and fact, warranting investigation 
rather than summary dismissal. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Abuse of dominance is not a disguised Section 3 claim 
‚û¢‚Äã Monsanto contended that the allegations essentially concerned contractual restrictions and should be examined 
only under Section 3 ‚Äì REJECTED 
‚û¢‚Äã Given the alleged 99% market share, absence of substitutes, and Monsanto‚Äôs vertical integration, the Court held 
that the context of dominance fundamentally alters the legal character of the conduct.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The same contractual clause, when imposed by a dominant firm, may constitute abuse under Section 4, 
even if similar clauses by non-dominant firms might only attract Section 3 scrutiny. 
‚û¢‚Äã The allegations under Sections 4(2)(a)(i), 4(2)(b), and 4(2)(e) were found to be independent and substantial, 
particularly regarding: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Foreclosure of independent R&D, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Restriction of technical development, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Leveraging dominance from upstream licensing into downstream seed markets. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Delhi High Court upheld the CCI‚Äôs prima facie order under Section 26(1) and refused to interfere at the threshold 
stage. 
153 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Court conclusively held that: 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI has jurisdiction to examine patent-related conduct where competition concerns arise. 
‚û¢‚Äã Bharti Airtel is not a blanket bar on CCI proceedings. 
‚û¢‚Äã Ericsson remains good law, affirming concurrent applicability of competition law. 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 3(5) does not protect unreasonable or excessive licensing conditions. 
‚û¢‚Äã The allegations warranted investigation, and CCI was competent to proceed. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Effectively, the Court reaffirmed that intellectual property rights are not competition-law free zones, and dominant patent 
holders remain subject to abuse of dominance scrutiny where licensing practices distort markets or suppress competition. 
 
Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson v CCI  [2023 | DHC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Multiple writ petitions and LPAs arose from proceedings initiated by the CCI against Ericsson, a holder of several 
telecom-related patents (including SEPs), based on allegations that Ericsson had imposed unfair and discriminatory licensing 
terms in violation of Sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act, 2002. 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI had earlier passed prima facie orders under Section 26(1) directing investigation into Ericsson‚Äôs licensing 
conduct.  
‚û¢‚Äã These proceedings were challenged on the ground that licensing of patents and imposition of conditions therein 
are comprehensively governed by Chapter XVI of the Patents Act, 1970, and therefore, the CCI lacked jurisdiction 
to inquire into such matters. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Notably, this case came against the backdrop of: 
‚û¢‚Äã the 2016 single judge decision in Ericsson v. CCI, which had upheld CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction, and 
‚û¢‚Äã the 2023 Monsanto decision, which reaffirmed that competition law applies concurrently to patent licensing. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Division Bench was therefore directly confronted with the institutional competence conflict between the Controller of 
Patents and the CCI, and the correctness of the earlier judicial approach. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether, once a patent is granted and the patentee asserts its rights, the CCI can inquire into the patentee‚Äôs conduct under 
Sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Chapter XVI of the Patents Act constitutes a complete and exclusive code for examining anti-competitive licensing 
conditions and abuse by patentees? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Competition Act or the Patents Act prevails in case of overlap, applying principles of statutory interpretation? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the 2016 Ericsson judgment and the 2023 Monsanto decision correctly appreciated the relationship between the 
two statutes? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether CCI proceedings survive once the underlying private dispute between the parties has been settled? 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Identifying the ‚Äúspecial law‚Äù: subject-matter based analysis 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court rejected the simplistic characterisation that the Competition Act is the special statute merely because it 
deals with competition, or that the Patents Act is special merely because it deals with patents ‚Üí instead, it adopted 
a subject-matter specific approach. 
‚û¢‚Äã The relevant subject matter here was ‚Äúimposition of conditions in patent licensing and abuse of rights by a 
patentee‚Äù, not competition law in the abstract.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
On this precise question, the Court found that Chapter XVI of the Patents Act is highly detailed, 
internally coherent, and purposefully structured to deal with unreasonable licensing conditions, market 
foreclosure, and abuse of patent monopoly. 
‚óè‚Äã
Thus, for inquiries into the exercise of patent rights, the Patents Act ‚Äî and not the 
Competition Act ‚Äî is the special statute. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Chapter XVI as a ‚Äúcomplete code‚Äù 
‚û¢‚Äã A central plank of the Court‚Äôs reasoning was that Chapter XVI constitutes a complete code governing: 
154 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
unreasonable conditions in licensing agreements, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
abuse of the exclusive rights conferred by a patent, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
public interest limitations on patent monopolies, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
remedies, including compulsory licensing. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court emphasised that Sections 83, 84, and 89 collectively mandate that patent rights: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
are not abused to stifle competition, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
are worked in India on reasonable terms, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
are exercised consistently with public interest. 
‚û¢‚Äã Importantly, the Court undertook a comparative analysis of the factors considered by: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
the CCI under Sections 19(3) and 19(4), and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
the Controller of Patents under Sections 84(6) and 84(7). 
‚û¢‚Äã It concluded that the substantive inquiries substantially overlap, particularly regarding market power, foreclosure, 
consumer interest, availability of alternatives, and effect on innovation. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Therefore, permitting the CCI to conduct a parallel inquiry would amount to duplication of jurisdiction, 
something the legislature could not have intended. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Legislative intent and later amendments 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court placed significant reliance on the temporal and textual evolution of the Patents Act. It noted that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Section 3(5) of the Competition Act was enacted with awareness of patent rights, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
subsequently, Section 84(6)(iv) was introduced into the Patents Act, explicitly empowering the Controller 
to examine anti-competitive practices in licensing. 
‚û¢‚Äã This, according to the Court, was particularly instructive of legislative intent.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Parliament, having enacted the Competition Act, still chose to strengthen the Patents Act‚Äôs internal 
competition safeguards, rather than referring such matters to the CCI. 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, even applying lex posterior derogat priori, the Patents Act must prevail in matters concerning inquiry 
into the conduct of patentees. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Rejection of Sections 21, 21A, and 62 as saving CCI jurisdiction 
‚û¢‚Äã Expressly overruled the 2016 Ericsson judgment ‚Üí misconstrued Sections 21 and 21A. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Sections 21 and 21A operate only where the Commission otherwise has jurisdiction ‚Üí cannot be used to 
create jurisdiction where it is otherwise excluded by legislative design. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Once the Court found that Chapter XVI exclusively occupies the field, there was no residual jurisdiction 
left for the CCI to exercise.  
‚óè‚Äã
Section 62 (‚Äúin addition to and not in derogation of‚Äù) cannot override a clear legislative intent of 
exclusion. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Overruling Monsanto (2023): resolving doctrinal inconsistency 
‚û¢‚Äã In direct contradiction to Monsanto (2023, SB), the Division Bench held that the assumption of concurrent 
jurisdiction was erroneous. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
While Monsanto proceeded on the basis that patent law and competition law address different harms, the 
present judgment held that where both statutes address the same harm in the same factual context, the 
specialised regime must prevail. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court thus implicitly rejects the ‚ÄúCCI always has jurisdiction‚Äù approach, restoring institutional primacy to 
the Controller of Patents in licensing disputes. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Settlement and collapse of CCI proceedings 
‚û¢‚Äã Independently, the Court held that since the informants and Ericsson had settled their disputes, the substratum of 
the CCI proceedings no longer survived.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Once the core grievance ceased to exist, continuation of investigation would serve no legitimate purpose 
‚Äî particularly when CCI was held to lack jurisdiction in the first place. 
 
Conclusion: The Delhi High Court (Division Bench) held that: 
155 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Patents Act, 1970 overrides the Competition Act, 2002 to the extent of inquiry into the exercise of patent rights and 
licensing conditions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Chapter XVI of the Patents Act is a complete code, conferring exclusive jurisdiction on the Controller of Patents. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The 2016 Ericsson judgment and the 2023 Monsanto decision are overruled on this point. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Sections 21, 21A, and 62 of the Competition Act do not preserve CCI jurisdiction where legislative intent indicates 
exclusion. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The CCI proceedings were quashed for want of jurisdiction, and additionally because the underlying dispute had been 
settled. 
 
JCB India Ltd. v. CCI [2024 | DHC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã JCB India Ltd. instituted a civil suit against BMPL alleging copyright infringement, piracy of registered designs, passing off, 
and allied IP violations relating to construction equipment. 
‚û¢‚Äã Granted an interim injunction in favour of JCB. 
‚û¢‚Äã BMPL, in response, challenged the validity of JCB‚Äôs registered designs by filing cancellation petitions before the 
Controller of Designs.  
‚û¢‚Äã Parallelly, negotiations commenced between the parties, and an interim arrangement was reached, with a 
settlement agreement under negotiation. 
‚ùñ‚Äã During the pendency of these IP disputes and negotiations, BMPL filed an Information under Section 19(1)(a), alleging that 
JCB had abused its dominant position by instituting and prosecuting allegedly vexatious IP litigation (characterised as ‚Äúbad 
faith / sham litigation‚Äù). 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI passed a prima facie order under Section 26(1) directing the DG to investigate.  
‚û¢‚Äã JCB challenged this order by way of a writ petition before the DHC and was initially granted protection.  
‚û¢‚Äã Despite this, the CCI sought and obtained search warrants from the CMM, Patiala House, under Section 41(8) of 
the Competition Act.  
‚û¢‚Äã JCB filed further writs challenging the search and seizure. 
‚ùñ‚Äã SC eventually set aside the Delhi High Court‚Äôs blanket injunction restraining the CCI investigation, but left open the 
jurisdictional question, including the permissibility of reliance on seized material. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Meanwhile, in the civil suit, BMPL filed an application under Order XII Rule 6 CPC, which was dismissed by the Delhi 
High Court.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Upon appeal,SC referred the dispute to mediation.  
‚û¢‚Äã The parties successfully resolved all disputes before the Delhi High Court Mediation Centre, and the settlement 
was taken on record by the Supreme Court. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Since BMPL had prayed for disposal of the CCI proceedings as part of the settlement, the Supreme Court directed the 
parties to approach the Delhi High Court for early disposal of the pending writ petitions.  
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, both parties jointly sought termination of the CCI proceedings. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether, once a comprehensive settlement has been arrived at between the parties through court-referred mediation, the 
CCI can continue proceedings concerning the same subject matter? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the initiation and prosecution of IP infringement litigation before a High Court can, by itself, constitute abuse of 
dominance under Section 4 as ‚Äúsham‚Äù or ‚Äúbad faith‚Äù litigation? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether post-settlement continuation of CCI proceedings undermines the finality and legitimacy of mediation under 
Indian law? 
‚ùñ‚Äã What is the scope of CCI‚Äôs powers post-settlement, including suo motu jurisdiction? 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Centrality of settlement and the ‚Äúspirit of mediation‚Äù 
156 
 
‚û¢‚Äã DB placed overwhelming emphasis on the role of mediation as a final and conclusive dispute resolution 
mechanism.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Court held that mediation is not merely an ancillary or preliminary step, but a substantive 
adjudicatory process recognised statutorily under Section 89 CPC and judicially endorsed by the SC. 
‚û¢‚Äã Allowing the CCI to continue proceedings after a settlement has been voluntarily and judicially recorded would: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
undermine the finality attached to mediation, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
risk prejudicing or unsettling the settlement, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
disincentivise parties from opting for mediation in future disputes. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court reasoned that if parties apprehend that regulatory proceedings may survive irrespective of settlement, 
the entire ADR framework would be rendered fragile and ineffective. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Subject matter identity and absence of public harm 
‚û¢‚Äã A critical aspect of the Court‚Äôs reasoning was that the subject matter of the CCI proceedings was identical to the 
subject matter of the settled IP dispute.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Information filed by BMPL did not raise any independent or systemic competition concern, but was 
entirely premised on JCB‚Äôs conduct in pursuing IP remedies before the Delhi High Court. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court expressly noted: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
no allegation that the settlement itself was abusive, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
no claim that the settlement had adverse effects on the market or consumers at large, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
no suggestion of a continuing or structural anti-competitive harm. 
‚û¢‚Äã In the absence of any public or market-wide impact, the Court held that CCI proceedings cannot be sustained 
merely to re-examine a private commercial dispute that has attained finality. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Abuse of judicial process ‚âÝ abuse of dominance (without more) 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI‚Äôs prima facie view was founded on the theory that JCB had abused judicial processes by filing IP 
infringement suits to harass competitors, amounting to abuse of dominance under Section 4. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court rejected this approach as overbroad and institutionally dangerous. It held that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
enforcement of statutory IP rights through courts is legitimate and legally protected conduct, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
every unsuccessful or aggressive IP action cannot be branded as ‚Äúsham litigation‚Äù, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
labelling High Court litigation as abusive would amount to second-guessing judicial processes. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court warned that converting routine IP enforcement into competition law violations would severely erode 
statutory IP regimes and impermissibly shift adjudicatory authority from constitutional courts to regulatory 
bodies. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Jurisdictional restraint vis-√Ý-vis IP disputes 
‚û¢‚Äã Reiterating themes seen in Ericsson and Monsanto (though in a distinct context), the Court held that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
questions relating to design validity, infringement, and enforcement lie squarely within the jurisdiction of 
civil courts and specialised IP authorities, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
the CCI must exercise institutional restraint when the same dispute is actively or conclusively adjudicated 
by a competent court. 
‚û¢‚Äã The attempt to ‚Äúhijack‚Äù an IP dispute from the High Court to the CCI was viewed with considerable scepticism, 
particularly where the dispute had already been settled. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Settlement does not extinguish all CCI powers ‚Äî important caveat 
‚û¢‚Äã Crucially, the Court did not hold that the settlement creates a blanket bar on CCI jurisdiction. In paragraph 55, 
the Court clarified that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
the CCI‚Äôs suo motu powers remain intact, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
the Commission may still act where independent information, systemic concerns, or abuse of dominance 
in the settlement itself are alleged. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, in the present case, no such allegation existed. The settlement was not challenged as exclusionary or 
abusive, and therefore fell outside the ambit of Section 4 scrutiny. 
‚û¢‚Äã This nuance distinguishes JCB from an absolute ‚Äúsettlement = no CCI‚Äù rule and preserves the Commission‚Äôs 
institutional role. 
157 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mediation as a constitutionally and statutorily protected process 
‚û¢‚Äã Relying on Salem Bar Association (2005), the Court emphasised that Parliament has consciously promoted ADR 
mechanisms to: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
reduce judicial backlog, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
preserve commercial relationships, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
ensure efficient dispute resolution. 
‚û¢‚Äã Post-settlement regulatory intervention, absent compelling public interest, would strip mediation of credibility 
and enforceability, contrary to legislative intent. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Once a comprehensive settlement has been arrived at through court-referred mediation, the CCI cannot continue 
proceedings concerning the same subject matter, absent allegations that the settlement itself is abusive or anti-competitive. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Continuation of CCI proceedings post-settlement undermines the spirit, finality, and legitimacy of mediation. 
‚ùñ‚Äã IP enforcement litigation before High Courts cannot, without more, be characterised as abuse of dominance or sham 
litigation. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The CCI‚Äôs general and suo motu powers are preserved, but were not attracted to the facts of this case. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Accordingly, the writ petitions were allowed and the CCI‚Äôs Section 26(1) order was set aside. 
 
Asian Paints Ltd. v Competition Commission of India [2025, Bom HC DB] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Asian Paints Ltd., a dominant player in the decorative paints market, challenged the assumption of jurisdiction by the CCI 
pursuant to an information filed by Birla Opus Paints under Section 19(1)(a) of the Competition Act, 2002, alleging abuse 
of dominance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Identical or substantially similar allegations had already been raised earlier in Case No. 36 of 2019 by JSW Paints and Balaji, 
where the CCI, after investigation, found no abuse of dominance or anti-competitive conduct by Asian Paints.  
‚û¢‚Äã On this basis, the Petitioner argued that under Section 26(2-A), the CCI was jurisdictionally barred from 
entertaining fresh information based on the same factual substratum. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Despite this, the CCI formed a prima facie opinion under Section 26(1) and directed the DG to conduct an investigation.  
‚û¢‚Äã Asian Paints approached the BHC, arguing that the CCI had failed to apply Section 26(2-A), acted arbitrarily, and 
violated principles analogous to res judicata. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Section 26(2-A) creates a jurisdictional bar preventing the CCI from entertaining subsequent information based 
on the same or substantially similar facts. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI was required to give reasons for not invoking Section 26(2-A) while directing investigation under Section 
26(1). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether an affected party has a right to hearing at the prima facie stage under Section 26(1). 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 26(1) [CCI to form a prima facie opinion and direct investigation by the DG] order is purely administrative, 
involving only the formation of a prima facie opinion, and does not determine rights or liabilities of parties.  
‚û¢‚Äã Consequently, such an order is not amenable to judicial review on merits, nor does it require prior hearing of the 
affected enterprise. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On the res judicata analogy, the Court categorically rejected the Petitioner‚Äôs contention that Section 26(2-A) incorporates a 
strict bar similar to res judicata. 
‚û¢‚Äã It grants the CCI discretion to close a case where the same or substantially similar issues have already been decided 
by it. 
‚û¢‚Äã Held that Section 26(2-A) does not create a jurisdictional embargo on the CCI.  
158 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Instead, it merely confers discretion on the Commission to avoid duplication of proceedings where issues 
have already been conclusively decided. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The earlier dismissal of the JSW/Balaji information was based on the absence of adequate material to 
substantiate allegations at that point in time.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Such dismissal does not imply that: 
‚óè‚Äã
no future information can ever be entertained, or 
‚óè‚Äã
subsequent informants are barred from approaching the CCI on similar issues. 
‚û¢‚Äã Subsequent information may be based on new facts, changed market conditions, fresh evidence, or altered 
competitive dynamics, particularly in dynamic markets.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Therefore, Section 26(2-A) cannot be read as freezing competition scrutiny permanently once an 
enterprise is exonerated in an earlier case. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Importantly, the Bench clarified the procedural consequence of Section 26(2-A): 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI is required to record reasons under Section 26(2-A) only when it chooses to close a case under Section 
26(2) or 26(2-A). 
‚û¢‚Äã Where the CCI decides not to close the case and instead directs investigation under Section 26(1), no obligation 
exists to justify non-application of Section 26(2-A). 
‚û¢‚Äã Thus, the absence of reasons on Section 26(2-A) in the impugned order did not render it illegal or arbitrary. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On the question of hearing, the Court reiterated settled law that no vested right of hearing exists at the prima facie stage.  
‚û¢‚Äã Whether to afford such a hearing is entirely within the discretion of the CCI, depending on facts and 
circumstances, and cannot be demanded as a matter of right. 
 
Conclusion: The BHC upheld the CCI‚Äôs order under Section 26(1) and dismissed the petition, holding that: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 26(2-A) does not operate as a res judicata bar, but merely grants discretionary power to the CCI. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The CCI was not required to record reasons for non-application of Section 26(2-A) when directing investigation. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Orders under Section 26(1) are administrative, preparatory, and do not attract principles of natural justice at that stage. 
‚ùñ‚Äã There was no illegality or jurisdictional error in the CCI directing a DG investigation. 
 
Zinc Carbon Dry Cell Batteries Cartel [2018 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Suo motu investigation by the CCI into alleged cartelisation in the market for zinc-carbon dry cell batteries in India.  
‚ùñ‚Äã OPs were major manufacturers and suppliers of zinc-carbon dry cell batteries.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Alleged that these manufacturers had entered into an agreement/understanding to control prices and distribution by 
coordinating an increase in the MRP. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The cartelisation was implemented through three broad mechanisms: 
‚û¢‚Äã coordinated price increases by manufacturers; 
‚û¢‚Äã active monitoring and enforcement of price discipline across the distribution chain; and 
‚û¢‚Äã reduction of price competition at the stockist, wholesaler, and retailer levels by agreeing upon and controlling the 
level of incentives and margins. 
‚ùñ‚Äã DG, during investigation, relied heavily on email communications evidencing coordination and identified key individuals 
involved in decision-making, proposing action under Section 48 ‚Üí concluded that the conduct amounted to contravention 
of Sections 3(3)(a), 3(3)(b), and 3(3)(c) read with Section 3(1). 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the conduct of the manufacturers constituted a cartel in violation of Section 3(3) read with Section 3(1) of the 
Competition Act, 2002. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether market allocation, price fixing, and output control were established through evidence. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether individuals and an industry association could be held liable under Section 48 of the Act. 
 
Application: 
159 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã The CCI found overwhelming evidence of elimination of price competition.  
‚û¢‚Äã Email exchanges between manufacturers clearly demonstrated coordination on price increases, timing, and 
implementation strategies.  
‚û¢‚Äã This coordination was not confined to the manufacturer level but was extended downstream, ensuring that 
wholesalers and retailers adhered to the agreed price structure, thereby eliminating intra-brand and inter-brand 
price competition. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Information sharing on sensitive commercial parameters such as operating margins, wholesale offer prices, and incentives ‚Üí 
such exchanges, in a concentrated market, were held to be inherently anti-competitive and incompatible with independent 
decision-making. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Evidence of market allocation, both geographically and by battery type, squarely attracting 3(3)(c).  
‚û¢‚Äã The cartel‚Äôs effectiveness was corroborated by stable market shares of the manufacturers over a six-year period 
(2010‚Äì11 to 2015‚Äì16), which the CCI treated as a strong indicator of cartel stability and success. 
‚ùñ‚Äã A crucial institutional facilitator was AIDCM, an industry association, which compiled and disseminated commercially 
sensitive data among members.  
‚û¢‚Äã While industry associations may legitimately represent common concerns, the present conduct crossed the line into 
facilitation of cartel behaviour, enabling monitoring and enforcement of the collusive arrangement. 
‚ùñ‚Äã In Section 48, the CCI clarified that liability is not confined to incorporated entities.  
‚û¢‚Äã Explanation (a) to Section 48 defines ‚Äúcompany‚Äù expansively to include firms and associations of individuals.  
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, AIDCM and its office bearers were held liable once contravention by the association was established.  
‚û¢‚Äã Senior management and key decision-makers of the manufacturing companies were also found to have played an 
active and knowing role in cartelisation. 
 
Conclusion: The CCI held that the manufacturers had entered into and implemented a cartel in violation of Sections 3(3)(a), (b), 
and (c) read with Section 3(1) of the Competition Act. The conduct resulted in complete foreclosure of price competition across the 
zinc-carbon dry cell battery market. 
Under Section 27, the CCI: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Directed the OPs to cease and desist from anti-competitive conduct; and 
‚ùñ‚Äã Imposed penalties at 10% of the average income of the manufacturers and individuals for the preceding three financial years. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The manufacturers admitted the existence of an understanding/arrangement, further reinforcing the finding of 
cartelisation.
